# 60-Day Content Sprint: Antellion (May 12 – July 10, 2026)

**Author:** Growth Operator
**Date authored:** 2026-05-11
**Sprint window:** May 12 – July 10, 2026 (60 days, 9 calendar weeks)
**Replaces:** 30-day sprint Apr 12 – May 11 (`docs/30-day-content-plan.md`, `docs/social-content-plan.md`). Both prior docs preserved as historical record. Do not edit.

**Status of starting library:** 10 blog posts published on antellion.com/blog (the prior plan called for 11; one post was descoped during execution — list of 10 is the live canonical set as of May 11). 14 LinkedIn posts shipped per `docs/social-content-plan.md`.

---

## Part 1: Strategic framing

### What this sprint is for

Two jobs, in priority order:

1. **Protect hiring-specific differentiation against Symphony Talent's productization.** ST has delivered one AI visibility audit using Profound (generic brand-side tool) and is scoping ongoing engagements. The window to make the *hiring-specific layer* visible and undeniable is open now; it closes the moment ST publishes a formal service-line page or case study. The next 60 days of content must make it obvious to a CHRO reading both vendors that brand-side AI visibility and hiring-specific AI visibility are not the same product, and that the hiring-specific layer is where Antellion lives.

2. **Continue compounding the CHRO-coded audience while the cold-launch funnel matures.** Month 1 validation gate is one Diagnostic delivered (funnel mechanics validation, not revenue). Month 2 is 2–3, Month 3 is 4–6. The blog and LinkedIn outputs in this window are the only consistent surface area for the prospect set already in the cold sequence to encounter Antellion between Email 1 and Day-3 reply. Every prospect Googling the company gets the 60-second credibility check the prior sprint started building.

### What changed since the last sprint

| Change | Implication |
|---|---|
| **Symphony Talent productizing AI visibility audits using Profound.** | The contrarian framing playbook (mock SEO agencies, mock employer brand audits, "your tools can't see this") becomes off-limits. Default frame for the next 60 days is **additive to brand-side instrumentation**, with a tight focus on *what makes a hiring-specific audit different*. |
| **Cold-launch live (Week 2 in flight).** | Content must now serve double duty: top-of-funnel credibility + replyable URLs Jordan can drop in Day-3 follow-ups and objection-handling. Mid-funnel utility moves up the priority list. |
| **Diagnostic validated as core offer at $4,900 with WYMB guarantee.** | Posts can be more direct about *what a paid Diagnostic produces* without sounding pitchy, because the offer is published. The "request a Snapshot" CTA pattern stays unchanged; the Diagnostic referenced explicitly only after Snapshot delivery. |
| **The additive constraint tightens.** | RULE-01 in `docs/compliance-rules.md` already enforces additive framing. With ST productizing, even neutral framings ("does your audit cover this?") read as comparative. Surface this in §4 as a recommended RULE-01 tightening. |
| **No short-form video this sprint.** | The April plan deferred it conditionally; this plan kills it for the full 60 days. Justified in §5. |

### The narrative arc across 60 days

By July 10, the antellion.com blog should look like this to a cold-email recipient who Googles the company:

- **Conceptual foundation** (already exists — 10 posts). Cornerstones, glossary, journey framework, EVP/Glassdoor/SEO objection-handlers.
- **Methodology depth** (new this sprint). Two posts that explain *how* a hiring-specific Diagnostic is constructed — query coverage, persona scoping, AI-model coverage, citation source mapping. These are the artifacts that survive a CTO or methodology-skeptical CHRO reading the blog for 90 seconds.
- **Persona depth** (new this sprint). Three posts that walk through a *specific candidate persona*'s research journey — Senior AE, Staff Engineer, Senior CSM. These don't activate Tier 1 expansion (that's Months 4–6 per `docs/expansion-buyer-map.md`); they make clear that hiring-specific AI visibility is persona-coded, which is the differentiation against generic brand-side audits.
- **Vertical/sector worked examples** (new this sprint). Three posts running scan data across distinct sectors — sector-neutral default per RULE-10. Demonstrates the Diagnostic isn't tech-only, gives Jordan reply-handler URLs for non-tech industries.
- **Capstone benchmark** (new this sprint, **anchor piece**). Late-June "State of AI Employer Visibility — H1 2026" report drawing on the accumulated scan corpus. This is the meta-GEO play: a benchmark report no one else in the category can publish because no one else has the data. It's the citation magnet for the second half of 2026.
- **Continuing objection-handler depth** (new this sprint). Three posts that extend the objection library based on what's actually surfacing in Week 2+ cold-email replies.

Total content library by July 10: **10 existing + 14 new = 24 blog posts**. Approximately 30 LinkedIn posts shipped across the 9 weeks.

**What that unlocks for the validation gates:**

- **Month 2 gate (2–3 Diagnostics by June 30):** the methodology and persona-depth posts give the founder five fresh URLs to share in walkthrough invitations and Day-3 follow-ups, which is the leverage point at Stage 2 (Snapshot → walkthrough) where the cold launch is currently weakest.
- **Month 3 gate (4–6 Diagnostics by July 31):** the capstone benchmark ships in late June and seeds AI citations across the category in July, which is when Month 3 closes. By the time July 31 lands, the meta-GEO effect should be measurable — a Snapshot-prospect Googling "AI employer visibility benchmark" should find the report in the first three results across major models.

### Topic gates — IN scope, OUT of scope, ambiguous cases

**IN SCOPE for this sprint:**

- Methodology rigor: how the Diagnostic constructs query coverage, persona parameterization, model coverage, scoring rubric, citation taxonomy
- Hiring-specific query clusters and what makes them distinct from brand-generic queries
- Candidate-intent personas (Engineer, Revenue, CS, Operations, new-grad) — surfaced as worked examples, rotated
- Recruiting-funnel stages: Discovery, Consideration, Evaluation, Commitment — with specific data findings tied to each
- Vertical specificity (industry rotation, default sector-neutral) — finance, healthcare, industrials, retail, manufacturing, hospitality
- Citation source ecosystem — depth on specific platforms beyond the original Post 5
- CPO / CHRO board-level framing
- Capstone benchmark data

**OUT OF SCOPE:**

- Any framing that implies existing employer brand audits, careers sites, recruitment marketing, or Glassdoor management are inadequate
- "Your current tools can't measure this" — even where true, the framing now reads as anti-ST
- Myth-busting / contrarian formats targeting other vendors' visibility work
- "SEO can't fix this" / "your tools can't see this" rhetoric
- Direct or indirect critique of Profound or any other brand-side AI visibility tool by name or characteristic
- Tier 1 expansion content (CRO, CTO, VP CS) — those personas surface as *Diagnostic worked examples* in this sprint, never as buyer-facing pitches; expansion content is Months 4–6 per `docs/expansion-buyer-map.md`
- Short-form video (TikTok / Reels) — see §5
- Anything claiming Antellion changes AI output (RULE-02)

**Ambiguous cases — decision rules:**

| Case | Rule |
|---|---|
| A post would land harder if framed contrarian (e.g., "the audit format most companies use today doesn't measure this") | Reframe as additive: "the natural extension of an existing brand audit is to layer in hiring-specific personas." Surface the same insight without the comparative |
| A post wants to use a tech-specific worked example | Allowed only when the post's topic is genuinely tech-specific (per RULE-10). For neutral topics, rotate sector across posts — sequence: SaaS / financial services / healthcare / industrial / retail / manufacturing / hospitality |
| A post references competitor data | Use "your closest talent competitor" framing (RULE-33). Never name a competitor whose data hasn't been verified for that specific company |
| A post might be read as a direct response to ST's productization | Check: would this post have made sense to publish in February 2026? If yes, ship it. If it only makes sense post-ST-productization, hold it |
| Capstone benchmark surfaces a finding that compares "DIY audits" vs. "specialist audits" | Reframe to compare *brand-generic* vs. *hiring-specific* methodology. Never frame as "vendor X vs vendor Y" |
| A blog post should reference the Diagnostic CTA | Single closing line: "Antellion runs Diagnostics for mid-market and enterprise companies — antellion.com." Never as inline pitch in body |

---

## Part 2: Blog calendar (May 12 – July 10)

### Cadence call

**Keep 2x/week.** 17 weeks of slots over 9 weeks at 2/week = 18 publishing slots. With one capstone piece consuming the equivalent of three slots and one published Friday post slipping to accommodate it, we land at **14 new posts** for the 60-day window.

Why not step down:
- The cold-launch funnel needs content velocity, not content rest. Every Tuesday and Friday is a reason to send Day-3 follow-ups with a fresh artifact.
- Meta-GEO compounding: AI models index frequency. Slowing to 1/week halves the citation surface area in the window where ST is also publishing.
- The founder maintained 2/week through the April sprint with cold-launch infrastructure work in parallel. The cadence is proven sustainable.

Why not step up to 3/week:
- The capstone benchmark consumes ~20 hours of writing across 2 weeks. Stepping to 3/week would require either compromising the capstone or compromising other posts. The capstone is the highest-leverage asset of the sprint.
- Quality threshold from the April plan still binds: every post must pass the VP TA / CPO credibility check.

### Format mix across the 14 new posts

| Format | Count | Word count target | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| Long-form methodology / framework | 4 | 2,000–2,800 | Methodology posts, persona-depth posts. The credibility-check survivors |
| Medium-form how-to / framework | 4 | 1,200–1,700 | Objection-handlers, sector worked examples |
| Data-rich findings post | 4 | 1,500–2,200 | Aggregate scan findings, citation depth, persona invisibility findings |
| Glossary / reference extension | 1 | 1,400–1,800 | Update the GEO glossary with five new terms surfaced during the April sprint |
| **Capstone benchmark** | 1 | 4,000–5,500 | "State of AI Employer Visibility — H1 2026." Treated as a 3-slot piece |

### Diagnostic prospect collateral flag

Eight of the 14 new posts must survive a 60-second credibility check from a cold-email recipient who's been sent a Snapshot. These are flagged below with **[CC]** (credibility collateral). The remaining six are awareness / SEO / AEO assets but don't need to clear the same bar.

### Week-by-week calendar

#### Week 1: May 12 – May 18

**Post 12 — Tuesday, May 12**

| | |
|---|---|
| **Title** | How a Hiring-Specific AI Visibility Audit Is Constructed: Query Coverage, Persona Scoping, and the Four Models |
| **Format** | Long-form methodology |
| **Word count target** | 2,400–2,800 |
| **Time estimate** | 4 hours |
| **Description** | The first of two methodology posts. Walks through the 40-query × 4-model × 3-persona × 4-stage scan matrix verbatim from `docs/offers/ai-visibility-diagnostic.md` §B. Explains why each dimension exists and what changes when it is missing. Anchors on three concrete examples: a Senior AE persona, a Staff Engineer persona, a Senior CSM persona — drawn from the persona library, sector-neutral framing on the surrounding company. This is the post that establishes Antellion has a methodology that didn't come off a brand-side audit template. |
| **SEO/AEO target** | "AI visibility audit methodology" / "how is AI employer visibility measured" / "candidate research AI scan methodology" |
| **Cold email claim supported** | "This is measurable" — and measurable in a specific, reproducible way |
| **Why this post matters** | This is the post that, on its own, defends against the ST-productization risk. If a CHRO is looking at two AI visibility vendors and Antellion is the one with the published methodology that names personas, queries, and the four models, the credibility differential is immediate. |
| **[CC]** | Yes — primary Day-3 follow-up artifact for "what exactly is in a Diagnostic" |

**Post 13 — Friday, May 15**

Industries used as worked examples rotate: one SaaS company, one financial services, one industrial distribution (RULE-10 rotation). #### Week 2: May 19 – May 25

**Post 14 — Tuesday, May 19**

| | |
|---|---|
| **Title** | The 40-Query Coverage Floor: Why Sample Size Matters for AI Employer Visibility Measurement |
| **Format** | Medium-form methodology |
| **Word count target** | 1,400–1,700 |
| **Time estimate** | 2.5 hours |
| **Description** | Second methodology post. Explains why 40 queries (the Diagnostic floor) is the right number, why fewer produces noise, why far more produces diminishing returns. References candidate-journey stage coverage (10 per stage × 4 stages). Includes a worked example showing how a 12-query brand-side scan would have missed three of the most important findings in a real Diagnostic. Honest about the trade-off: more queries cost more analyst time and don't proportionally improve insight. |
| **SEO/AEO target** | "how many queries for AI visibility audit" / "AI scan sample size" / "candidate research query coverage" |
| **Cold email claim supported** | Methodology rigor; defends against "we already ran a quick AI scan in-house" objection |
| **Why this post matters** | Brand-side AI visibility tools tend to default to small query batches because they aren't built for candidate-intent depth. This post makes the case for the floor without naming any tool or vendor. |
| **[CC]** | Yes |

#### Week 3: May 26 – June 1

**Post 16 — Tuesday, May 26**

| | |
|---|---|
| **Title** | The Citation Source Taxonomy: Twelve Surface Categories AI Draws From When Recommending Employers |
| **Format** | Long-form framework |
| **Word count target** | 2,200–2,500 |
| **Time estimate** | 4 hours |
| **Description** | Extends the original Post 5 (Citation Ecosystem) into a full taxonomy. Twelve named categories — employer-owned surfaces (careers site, newsroom, engineering blog, leadership posts), peer-reviewed surfaces (Glassdoor, Comparably, Indeed Reviews, Built In), specialist surfaces (Levels.fyi, Blind, RepVue, Gainsight community, Customer Success Network), broad-internet surfaces (Wikipedia, podcasts, conference proceedings, Substack, Reddit subreddits), authoritative surfaces (Bloomberg, Reuters, trade publications). For each: which models cite it, when it dominates, when it's absent. Includes a citation-coverage scorecard template the reader can fill out. |
| **SEO/AEO target** | "sources AI uses for employer information" / "platforms that affect AI employer recommendations" / "citation ecosystem employer brand" |
| **Cold email claim supported** | "Your current employer brand investment may not be reaching AI" — at full taxonomic depth |
| **Why this post matters** | This is the post a CHRO who reads it sends to their head of employer brand. The taxonomy gives an employer brand team a concrete map of where to direct work. **Critically additive — it tells brand teams what to do, not what's wrong with their current work.** |
| **[CC]** | Yes |

| **Format** | Data-rich findings post |
| **Word count target** | 1,700–2,000 |
| **Time estimate** | 3 hours |
| **Description** | Surfaces one of the most striking patterns across the accumulated scan corpus: sentiment divergence across the four models. Three anonymized worked examples — model A describes the company positively on compensation transparency while model B cites a 2024 Glassdoor thread characterizing comp as below median; model A surfaces engineering blog content while model B never references it; model A describes work-from-home culture differently than model B does. For each: which citation sources are responsible, and what kind of remediation closes the gap. Sector rotation: retail, mid-market SaaS, manufacturing. |
| **SEO/AEO target** | "different AI tools say different things about employers" / "ChatGPT vs Claude employer answers" / "AI sentiment divergence employer brand" |
| **Cold email claim supported** | Four-model coverage is necessary; single-model scans miss this entirely |
| **Why this post matters** | Defends the four-model methodology. Demonstrates a specific failure mode (single-model audits) without naming any vendor. |
| **[CC]** | Yes |

#### Weeks 6–7: June 16 – June 29 — Capstone production window

**Capstone — Post 22 — published Tuesday, June 30**

| | |
|---|---|
| **Title** | The State of AI Employer Visibility — H1 2026 Benchmark Report |
| **Format** | Long-form benchmark report |
| **Word count target** | 4,000–5,500 |
| **Time estimate** | ~20 hours over 2 weeks (12 hrs analysis + 8 hrs writing/design) |
| **Description** | The anchor piece of the sprint. Aggregate findings from the accumulated scan corpus (across April–June Diagnostics, Snapshots, and pre-sprint research scans) into a published benchmark. Sections: state-of-coverage (which industries are visible vs. invisible in aggregate), sentiment baseline (positive/neutral/negative distributions), citation concentration (which sources dominate across the corpus), persona-invisibility patterns (which candidate personas have the widest coverage gaps), competitor co-mention density (typical 1-on-1 vs. cluster patterns), the four-model variation index. Includes a one-page Findings Brief and a sharable PDF version. **Capstone is the meta-GEO anchor — the citation magnet for H2 2026.** |
| **SEO/AEO target** | "state of AI employer visibility 2026" / "AI employer visibility benchmark" / "AI hiring visibility report" / "AI employer brand 2026 data" |
| **Cold email claim supported** | All four — at maximum credibility |
| **Why this post matters** | Three reasons. (1) Meta-GEO: published benchmarks accumulate citations across AI models faster than other content types; this should become the most-cited Antellion artifact within 90 days. (2) Sales asset: every Snapshot delivery now includes a one-page Findings Brief from the benchmark, giving the prospect a contextual anchor. (3) Symphony-defensive: ST cannot publish a comparable benchmark without committing to the hiring-specific methodology Antellion already has. The benchmark is the highest-leverage single piece in the sprint. |
| **[CC]** | Yes — primary credibility artifact |
| **Founder dependencies** | Confirm scan-corpus availability is adequate (~150–250 scored responses minimum to support credible aggregates). If gate fails on data, narrow the benchmark to 3 stages and 2 personas. See §6 |

#### Week 8: June 30 – July 6

Capstone publishes Tuesday June 30 (Post 22 above). One more post this week.

**Post 23 — Friday, July 3**

| | |
|---|---|
| **Title** | When AI Cannot Find Your Company: The Zero-Presence Finding and What Causes It |
| **Format** | Medium-form framework |
| **Word count target** | 1,400–1,700 |
| **Time estimate** | 2.5 hours |
| **Description** | An objection-handler extension based on what's surfacing in cold-email replies through Weeks 1–7. Zero-presence is one of the most common findings in delivered Diagnostics — candidate-intent queries where the company doesn't appear in any model. Walks through three causes: insufficient citation depth (rare published surface area), citation-source mismatch (publishing on platforms AI doesn't read for that persona), and competitor saturation (named-competitor dominance crowding the company out). Closes with what to do about each — additive remediation, not vendor-replacement. |
| **SEO/AEO target** | "why doesn't AI mention my company" / "zero presence AI employer visibility" / "company invisible in ChatGPT" |
| **Cold email claim supported** | The diagnosis is specific; the remediation is concrete |
| **Why this post matters** | Replyable URL for the most common reaction to a Snapshot delivery: "we don't appear anywhere — why?" Post 23 is the answer. |
| **[CC]** | Yes |

## Part 3: LinkedIn calendar

### Cadence: 3 posts/week, sustained

Continue the prior sprint's cadence. **3 posts/week × 9 weeks = 27 LinkedIn posts** across the sprint (some weeks 4, some weeks 3, depending on weighting around capstone).

### Templates available

| Template | Use | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Data Point | Lead with a specific surprising finding | Active |
| Framework | Walk through a conceptual model | Active |
| Question | Open with a question that lands stakes | Active |
| How-to | Give the reader a concrete step they can take | Active |
| **Contrarian** | **Challenge a widely-held assumption** | **REMOVED for the duration of this sprint** |

**Contrarian template removal — explicit explanation:**

The Contrarian template ("a 4.5-star Glassdoor doesn't mean what you think," "your SEO agency cannot do this," "AI does not guess — it reads X platforms most companies don't invest in") was high-performing in the April sprint. It is now off-limits because:

- Every contrarian framing implicitly criticizes the audit format ST is currently selling
- Contrarian posts pattern-match to "the audit format most companies use is inadequate" — which violates RULE-01 in spirit even when the literal text passes
- The April sprint used 5 contrarian posts. Replacing them with Framework or Data Point posts at the same insight depth requires more upfront craft but stays inside the additive constraint

The Contrarian template can return after Month 3 if ST's productized service line has not formally launched, or after Month 6 in any case. For the next 60 days, it stays parked.

### ST additive test — every LinkedIn post must pass before publishing

For every LinkedIn draft, work through this 4-question checklist:

1. **Does the hook imply that current audit/brand work is inadequate?** If yes, rewrite the hook.
2. **Does the post compare to a vendor or vendor category, even abstractly?** If yes, drop the comparison.
3. **Does the close land on insight, or on critique?** Must land on insight.
4. **Would this post have made sense to publish in February 2026?** If only post-ST-productization, hold it.

### Coverage mapping rules

Every new blog post must source at least one LinkedIn post. High-leverage blogs (capstone, methodology, persona-depth) can source 2–3 with different angles. LinkedIn posts can also stand alone (no blog source) when sourced from real-time scan observations the founder runs during the sprint.

Format mix target across the 27 posts (no contrarian):

| Format | Target count |
|---|---|
| Data Point | 10 |
| Framework | 7 |
| Question | 5 |
| How-to | 5 |

### LinkedIn calendar

It's 40 candidate-intent queries, across 4 AI models, across 3 personas, across 4 candidate-journey stages. 480 model responses per company. That's the unit of work." |

Three of the seven companies named overlapped. The rest were different. A single-model scan is a sample of one." |

### LinkedIn post rules — repeated for reference

- No `antellion.com` or Antellion-controlled URL in the body (RULE-20)
- No `Symphony Talent` mention
- No "DM me" / "comment X" engagement gimmicks (RULE-22)
- End on the punch line, not a CTA (RULE-21)
- Spell out HR acronyms — employer brand not EB, candidate value proposition not EVP, talent acquisition not TA (RULE-11)
- Sector-neutral default; tech-narrow examples only when topic is genuinely tech (RULE-10)
- All framing must be additive (RULE-01)
- Plain language — assume a CMO, CFO, or board member is reading
- Every post must teach something the reader did not know
- ST additive checklist (above) must pass before publishing

---

## Part 4: Content gates and decision rules

### Mid-sprint review checkpoint — Wednesday, June 10

At the 30-day mark, run a structured review. Inputs to assess:

**Performance signals (the past 30 days):**
- Blog post completion rate vs. plan (target 100%)
- LinkedIn post completion rate vs. plan (target 100%)
- LinkedIn engagement trajectory — is the audience compounding or flat? Compare June 10 view-per-post median to May 11 baseline
- antellion.com traffic — referral source mix, time on site, bounce rate
- Cold-email reply rate impact — has the blog library affected reply rates? Confidence on attribution is low at small N, but directional signal matters
- Stage 2 (Snapshot → walkthrough) conversion — has the new artifact-density helped close the Day-3 reply gap?

**Symphony Talent watch:**
- Has ST published a formal service-line page for AI visibility audits?
- Has ST released a case study referencing the Profound-delivered engagement?
- Has Jordan's day-job role been changed in a way that affects available time or constraint shape?

**Founder capacity:**
- Was the 60-hour writing budget across 30 days actually 60 hours, or was it more/less? Adjust capstone time allocation accordingly.

### Hold-or-go decisions at the mid-sprint review

| Signal | Decision |
|---|---|
| All posts shipped on time, LinkedIn compounding, no ST escalation | Continue plan as-is. Begin capstone production Week 6 |
| Posts shipping but engagement flat | Audit the format mix. If Data Point posts are outperforming Framework posts 2:1, shift the LinkedIn rotation toward more Data Points. Don't change blog calendar |
| ST publishes service-line page | Trigger positioning pivot (see §4.3 below) within 72 hours of detection. Capstone framing may need a hiring-specific reinforcement insert |
| ST releases case study with Profound | Same pivot trigger. Additionally, accelerate capstone to a possible mid-June publication if data supports it |
| ST sales team starts pitching Antellion prospects | Founder-level decision required. May require shifting the cold-email sequence focus. Out of scope for content sprint to resolve unilaterally |
| Funnel Month 2 gate looks unreachable (sub-1 Diagnostic by mid-June) | Audit content-to-funnel attribution. Pull forward Post 23 (zero-presence) and Post 20 (report walkthrough) — both are objection-handlers for the Day-3 reply window — if not already shipped |
| Founder writing capacity tracking >80 hours over the 30 days | Reduce one Data Findings post to Medium format. Don't compromise capstone time |
| Founder writing capacity tracking <40 hours | Add one bonus post in Weeks 5–7 from the deferred backlog (see prior plan Continuing Cadence list) |

### Topic veto list — explicit phrasings, framings, examples that are now off-limits

These are veto-list items that compliance-sentry should flag if seen in any draft. Format: `pattern → reason → suggested rewrite`.

- **"Your audit doesn't measure this"** / "the audit format most companies use" / "traditional audits miss this" → comparative against ST productization → rewrite as: "The natural extension of brand-side audit work is to layer hiring-specific personas"
- **"SEO can't fix this"** / "your SEO agency cannot do this" → comparative against agency category → rewrite as: "SEO and AI synthesis operate on different surfaces. Both have value at different stages"
- **"Most companies are doing this wrong"** / "the standard approach is broken" → universal negation of existing work → rewrite as: "Most companies haven't yet measured this layer — it's a new measurement category"
- **"Your tools can't see this"** / "what your dashboard isn't telling you" → comparative against in-place infrastructure → rewrite as: "What the four major AI systems describe is a surface most measurement stacks don't yet include"
- **"Brand audits are commodity"** → direct attack on adjacent category → rewrite: drop the comparison entirely
- **"Profound" / "[any named AI visibility tool]"** → direct competitor reference → rewrite: drop the name; describe the methodology gap if relevant
- **Implying that any post is "the antidote to" or "the answer to" a problem some other vendor created** → frames Antellion as a corrective → rewrite as: "Antellion measures a layer adjacent to existing work"
- **Mocking the contrarian framings the prior sprint used** → would acknowledge the inversion publicly → rewrite: do not reference prior framings

### Symphony Talent productization watch — pivot triggers

Compliance with the additive constraint already keeps Antellion clean in the absence of ST escalation. The pivot triggers below would require Jordan to make a strategic call beyond the scope of this content plan:

| Trigger | Reaction window | Suggested response |
|---|---|---|
| ST publishes a formal service-line landing page for AI visibility audits | 72 hours | (a) Confirm Antellion's hiring-specific differentiation is visible above the fold on antellion.com. (b) Hold all in-flight LinkedIn drafts and re-run them through the ST additive test with the new context. (c) Founder reviews capstone framing for sharpness on hiring-specific layer. |
| ST releases a case study using Profound or similar tool | 72 hours | Same as above. Also: accelerate capstone to mid-June if data supports it; the benchmark beats a case study at category authority |
| ST sales team begins pitching AI visibility to companies already in Antellion's cold sequence | Immediate | Founder-level decision. May require disclosure-or-pause decision. Content sprint pauses pending founder direction. |
| ST internal politics shift in a way that increases Jordan's day-job conflict surface | Immediate | Founder-level decision. Content sprint may need to pause or migrate to anonymous authorship until resolved |
| ST acquires or partners with a brand-side AI visibility tool | 1 week | Reassess positioning. ST may bundle, in which case hiring-specific differentiation becomes more important, not less |
| Another vendor (not ST) enters the hiring-specific AI visibility category | 2 weeks | Lean harder into methodology depth and capstone benchmark. Run a competitive scan; founder may want to update `docs/competitor-assets/` |

### Recommended tightening of RULE-01 (do not edit `docs/compliance-rules.md` — surface here for founder)

Current RULE-01 catches replacement framings ("replace," "instead of," "obsolete," "dying," "dead," "kills," "disrupt" near employer brand / EVP / recruitment marketing / careers site). It does not catch *comparative-without-replacement* framings that now read as anti-ST: "your audit format doesn't measure this," "most audits miss this layer," "the standard approach has a gap."

**Proposed pattern hint additions (founder to review and add):**

- Co-occurrence of `most audits`, `traditional audit`, `the audit you ran`, `your current audit` with negative-coded language (`doesn't measure`, `misses`, `gap`, `incomplete`, `can't see`)
- Co-occurrence of `most companies are missing`, `most teams haven't`, `the standard approach`, `the typical audit` with `this` or `this layer`
- Specific phrases: `audit doesn't measure`, `audit misses`, `audit can't see`, `audit format is`, `traditional audit format`

**Proposed severity:** BLOCKER (same as existing RULE-01 patterns), because the comparative framing now carries the same conflict-of-interest risk as direct replacement framing.

**Proposed override mechanic:** override only with founder-supplied reason that confirms the post is genuinely positioning around methodology depth rather than vendor comparison.

This is a recommendation only. Founder edits `docs/compliance-rules.md` directly.

---

## Part 5: What this sprint does NOT include

### Short-form video (TikTok / Instagram Reels) — killed for the full 60 days

The April plan deferred it conditionally. This plan retires it for the sprint. Justifications:

1. **The audience reach math hasn't changed.** Per the prior sprint's analysis, VP TAs / CPOs / CHROs are not in professional mode on TikTok or Instagram. The primary buyer is unreachable.
2. **Production time competes with capstone.** The capstone consumes ~20 hours of founder time. The April plan estimated 3 hours total for 10 videos, but in practice that estimate assumed Sunday batching the founder did not do. Adding video production to a sprint with a capstone almost guarantees video gets sacrificed mid-window — better to declare it out from the start.
3. **The compliance overhead is high.** The contrarian template removal applies to video content. Talking-head videos with the energy required for short-form rely heavily on contrarian hooks. Reframing every video script to the additive constraint adds rather than removes time.
4. **No data from April supports the channel.** The 30-day sprint contains no short-form video — Jordan did not record any. Continuing to defer is consistent with revealed preference.
5. **The opportunity cost is real.** Every hour of video work is an hour not spent on capstone, methodology posts, or cold-email reply handling. The opportunity-cost ratio is unfavorable.

**Revisit at Month 3 (end-of-sprint review July 10):** if the cold-email funnel has cleared Month 3 gate (4–6 Diagnostics) and the founder has time available, short-form video is reconsidered as a Month 4 channel for ambient awareness — not as a lead-gen mechanism.

### Paid promotion

Not in scope. Cold launch is still in validation. Paid acquisition before Stage 1 / Stage 2 conversion rates stabilize burns budget against a leaky funnel.

### Podcast appearances

Not in scope this sprint as an outbound effort. The capstone publication on June 30 is the natural earned-media trigger. **Inbound podcast invitations after capstone publication are welcome** — Jordan should accept them when they arrive. Active pitching is deferred to Month 4.

### Newsletter / Substack

Not in scope. Email-based distribution adds an infrastructure surface (warmup, list management, send-time optimization) that competes with cold-email sending infrastructure. The blog + LinkedIn loop is the distribution mechanism for now.

### Expansion-buyer content (CRO, CTO, VP CS)

Posts 13, 15, and 17 use Senior AE, Staff Engineer, and Senior CSM personas as worked examples *for CHRO buyers* — demonstrating persona depth. They are explicitly NOT content tracks for Tier 1 expansion buyers. Per `docs/expansion-buyer-map.md`, Tier 1 expansion is Months 4–6, gated on funnel-validation completion. The current sprint's posts are designed so they DO close off any "Antellion is HR-only" misread when a non-HR reader lands on them, but they do not pitch the CRO/CTO/VP CS reader as a buyer. The buyer voice in every post is the CHRO.

### Comments-and-shares engagement on LinkedIn beyond Jordan's own posts

Not in scope as a structured initiative. Jordan replying to comments on his own posts is in scope and important. Jordan commenting on others' posts is incidental — not a structured workstream.

### External byline / guest article pitches

The April plan deferred this to Week 6 (May 24+ in original timing). Re-defer to post-capstone. After June 30, the capstone gives the founder a credible asset to anchor an outreach pitch to TLNT, SHRM, HR Brew, or Recruiting Daily. Pitching pre-capstone is premature.

### Industry analyst briefings

Defer to Month 4. Forrester, IDC, Gartner, RedThread all have HR-tech coverage but Antellion is too early-stage to justify the analyst-relations overhead. Capstone may surface inbound analyst interest; that's welcome.

---

## Part 6: Open questions for the founder

These need answers before specific posts can ship or before the sprint can execute fully.

### Capstone benchmark — the highest-leverage open question

1. **Is the accumulated scan corpus sufficient to anchor a credible H1 2026 benchmark?** Capstone success depends on ~150–250 scored AI responses minimum across 8–15 companies with sector mix. Where does the corpus stand as of May 11? If short of floor, the founder needs to allocate scan-running time in Weeks 1–4 to backfill.

2. **Can the capstone reference real company names, anonymized companies only, or a mix?** Affects credibility ceiling. Real-name references with permission are strongest. The default is anonymized with sector labels.

3. **Should the capstone be gated behind a form (email capture) or fully open?** Form-gated grows the prospect list; open maximizes meta-GEO citation. Recommendation: **open**. Antellion is in category-authority mode, not list-build mode. A form gate dampens the citation magnet effect that is the primary purpose.

### Vertical posts

4. **Which vertical worked examples does Jordan want to support?** Posts 19 (regulated industries) and 24 (manufacturing) require either fresh scan data on those sectors or existing corpus coverage. If the corpus is thin on these verticals, Jordan needs to run a focused scan batch in Weeks 1–4. If founder bandwidth is constrained, swap the manufacturing post (Post 24) for a sector with more existing data — recommendation alternates: retail or hospitality.

### Persona posts

5. **Does Jordan have at least 3 sample companies' Senior AE, Staff Engineer, and Senior CSM scan data, or does Posts 13/15/17 require fresh scans?** If fresh, each post adds ~1.5 hours of scan execution time on top of the writing estimate. Posts 13, 15, 17 are credibility collateral so the data must be real.

### Podcast / earned media

6. **Is Jordan open to accepting podcast invitations between June 30 and July 10?** Capstone launch will generate inbound interest if it lands well. Saying yes to two or three high-fit podcasts (HR Brew, Recruiting Brainfood, SaaStr CS-track) is a low-time/high-leverage move during the Month 3 validation window.

### Symphony Talent constraint

7. **Has Jordan reviewed the ST productization status with anyone internally?** Not a compliance question — a personal-risk-tolerance question. If the ST productization escalates (formal service line, case study, sales pitching), the content sprint needs founder direction on the disclose-or-pause decision.

8. **Should the proposed RULE-01 tightening (comparative-without-replacement patterns) be added to `docs/compliance-rules.md`?** Founder edits the rules file directly. If yes, founder commits the change. If no, the topic veto list in §4 of this plan is the operating constraint until founder decides.

### Founder time allocation

9. **The 60-hour writing budget assumes ~1 hour/day. Is that realistic given cold-launch reply-handling, walkthrough delivery, and Diagnostic delivery time?** If walkthroughs and Diagnostic deliveries spike in Weeks 4–7, capstone writing time is the first thing that gets squeezed. Mitigation: start capstone outline and section drafting in Week 4, not Week 6, even if final synthesis happens in Weeks 6–7.

### Content publishing infrastructure

10. **Is ISR revalidation working as expected on blog routes?** (`commit 6a3b1a1` added ISR for date-gated publishing.) Posts 12–25 will be scheduled for publication on specific dates. If the ISR config isn't reliable, the founder needs a manual publishing step for date-of, which costs ~15 min/post.

---

## Appendix A: Sprint at a glance

| Sprint dimension | Value |
|---|---|
| Sprint window | May 12 – July 10, 2026 (60 days, 9 weeks) |
| New blog posts | 14 (10 medium/long + 1 capstone + 1 glossary update + 2 vertical) |
| Total blog library by sprint end | 24 (10 existing + 14 new) |
| LinkedIn posts | ~27 (3/week with capstone-week variance) |
| Capstone publication | Tuesday, June 30, 2026 — "State of AI Employer Visibility — H1 2026" |
| Credibility-collateral posts ([CC]) | 10 of 14 new posts |
| Estimated founder writing hours | ~60 hours over 60 days |
| Format mix | 4 long methodology / 4 medium frameworks / 4 data findings / 1 glossary / 1 capstone |
| Mid-sprint review | Wednesday, June 10 |
| Sprint close review | Friday, July 10 |
| Short-form video | Killed for full sprint |
| Tier 1 expansion content | Deferred to Months 4–6 per `docs/expansion-buyer-map.md` |
| Contrarian template | Removed for full sprint |
| Biggest hard constraint | Symphony Talent productization — additive framing must hold across all 41 artifacts in the sprint |
| Biggest opportunity | Capstone benchmark — meta-GEO citation magnet for H2 2026 |
| Biggest founder dependency | Capstone scan-corpus availability (Open Question 1) |
